verdict: All three LinkedIn posts and the newsletter meet the depth and specificity standard. Post 1 (anchoring) has the strongest hook and the clearest operator application. Post 2 (social vs. market norms) carries the most retention value and the script is field-realistic. Post 3 (endowment effect) cleanly dismantles the 'self-managing owners are just cheap' misdiagnosis. The newsletter extends all three posts with meaningful additional material, not just summaries.
strengths: Each post attacks a specific false belief rather than summarizing a concept
Scripts in posts 2 and 3 sound like something a real operator would say in a real appointment
Field notes are owner-type specific and add information not already in the body copy
Newsletter introduces two additional concepts (power of free, options/decision paralysis) not covered in the posts, and builds a usable behavioral sequence
Post 3 hook ('Self-managing owners are not cheap. They're endowed.') is the strongest single line in the batch and will stop the scroll
problems: Post 1 is near the character limit and could trim the 'Practical moves' section to tighten the argument
Post 2's transition from norm theory to the maintenance call scenario is clear but could be one sentence shorter in the setup
required_fixes: None blocking. Post 1 character count should be verified before publishing.
